faculty.name: Thomas Vandervelde
faculty.title: Professor and Chair of Electrical and Computer Engineering; Director of TIAMAT
faculty.summary: Director of TIAMAT (Tufts Interdisciplinary Advanced Materials Center) and Program Chair. Studies light-matter interactions in nanostructured materials, semiconductor photonics, and epitaxial crystal growth.

faculty.name: David Kaplan
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor of Biomedical Engineering
faculty.summary: Steering committee member. Distinguished Professor and pioneer in silk-based biomaterials, tissue engineering, and biopolymer design.

faculty.name: Peggy Cebe
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Steering committee member. Studies polymer physics, crystallization behavior, and thermal properties of polymeric and biopolymeric materials.

faculty.name: Igor Sokolov
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Steering committee member. Studies nanomaterials, surfaces, and their mechanical and biological properties using scanning probe microscopy.

faculty.name: Art Utz
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Steering committee member. Studies molecular reactions at surfaces, heterogeneous catalysis, and energy transfer dynamics at interfaces.

faculty.name: Tim Atherton
faculty.title: Associate Professor and Chair of Physics & Astronomy
faculty.summary: Department Chair. Studies soft condensed matter including colloids, liquid crystals, and self-assembly using computational methods.

faculty.name: Ayse Asatekin
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Chemical and Biological Engineering
faculty.summary: Designs advanced polymer membranes for separation processes, water purification, and surface engineering applications.

faculty.name: Matt Panzer
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Chemical and Biological Engineering
faculty.summary: Develops gel electrolytes, stretchable ionics, and soft materials for energy storage and electronic device applications.

faculty.name: Prashant Deshlahra
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Chemical and Biological Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies heterogeneous catalysis for sustainable chemicals and fuels using DFT calculations and kinetic modeling.

faculty.name: Charles Sykes
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Uses scanning tunneling microscopy to study surface chemistry and single-atom catalysis at the atomic scale.

faculty.name: Krishna Kumar
faculty.title: Robinson Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Designs proteins and peptides with novel chemical functionalities for biomaterials and therapeutic applications.

faculty.name: Mary Jane Shultz
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Studies molecular interactions at surfaces and interfaces, especially ice and environmentally relevant interfaces, using nonlinear spectroscopy.

faculty.name: Aseema Mohanty
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering
faculty.summary: Develops integrated photonic devices and circuits using silicon and novel materials platforms.

faculty.name: Sameer Sonkusale
faculty.title: Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering
faculty.summary: Develops flexible and stretchable electronic devices for biomedical sensing and wearable applications.

faculty.name: Luisa Chiesa
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies superconducting materials for sustainable energy applications, including material characterization at cryogenic temperatures.

faculty.name: Marc Hodes
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies transport phenomena at surfaces, superhydrophobic coatings, and thermal management materials.

faculty.name: Mark Kachanov
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies the mechanics of heterogeneous materials, microstructure-property relationships, and fracture mechanics.

faculty.name: Peter Love
faculty.title: Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Studies quantum computing and quantum simulation algorithms with applications to materials science and chemistry.

faculty.name: Cristian Staii
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Physics
faculty.summary: Uses scanning probe microscopy to study biophysics and nanomechanics of neuronal growth and cellular materials.

faculty.name: Fio Omenetto
faculty.title: Professor of Biomedical Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies silk-based biomaterials for applications in biophotonics, flexible electronics, and sustainable materials.

faculty.name: Doug Matson
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies solidification processing of metals and alloys, casting technology, and materials processing.

faculty.name: Luis Dorfmann
faculty.title: Professor of Civil and Environmental Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies nonlinear mechanics of soft materials including magneto-electro-active polymers, rubber elasticity, and biomechanics.

faculty.name: Paul Simmonds
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies epitaxial growth of III-V semiconductor nanostructures including quantum dots for photonic and electronic applications.

faculty.name: Rob White
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Develops MEMS devices, microfabrication processes, and thin-film sensor technologies.

faculty.name: James Adler
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Develops numerical methods and scientific computing approaches for complex fluids, plasma physics, and materials modeling.

funding.note: Unknown/Verify

faculty.name: Carlos Bustamante
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses single-molecule manipulation techniques, especially optical tweezers, to study the mechanics of DNA, RNA, and proteins, investigating how molecular motors generate force and how individual proteins fold and unfold.

faculty.name: Ahmet Yildiz
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how motor proteins transport cargo along microtubules using single-molecule biophysics and cryo-EM to understand dynein motility, bidirectional transport, and the role of microtubule-associated proteins in intracellular trafficking.

faculty.name: Jennifer Doudna
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Pioneered CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing technology; her lab discovers and determines mechanisms of novel CRISPR-Cas proteins and develops genome editing applications for use in vitro, in plants, and in mammals.

faculty.name: Eva Nogales
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates the molecular machinery behind cytoskeleton dynamics (particularly microtubules in cell division) and gene expression regulation using cryo-electron microscopy to visualize macromolecular assemblies at near-atomic resolution. HHMI Investigator and 2025 Shaw Prize laureate.

faculty.name: Susan Marqusee
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates how the linear sequence of amino acids encodes complex three-dimensional shapes that carry out cellular functions, using biophysical, structural, and computational techniques to understand protein folding, energy landscapes, and dynamics.

faculty.name: James Hurley
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates how protein-membrane interactions control cell and organelle shape and signaling, with focus on autophagy mechanisms, mitophagy in Parkinson's disease, lysosome biology in cancer and Alzheimer's, and viral hijacking of membrane traffic.

faculty.name: Jay Groves
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates how spatial organization influences signal transduction at the cell membrane, combining optical microscopy, materials fabrication, and cell biology to study biochemical processes down to the single-molecule level, with current focus on two-dimensional protein condensation phase transitions in T cell and growth factor receptor signaling.

faculty.name: Daniel Fletcher
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops optical and force microscopy tools and microfabricated devices to study the mechanical properties of cells, including smartphone-based microscopes for medical diagnostics and platforms for studying cytoskeletal dynamics.

faculty.name: Ehud Isacoff
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates synaptic signaling, sensory perception, and circuit dynamics using advanced imaging, electrophysiology, and genetic engineering; renowned for developing fluorescent sensors that visualize and control neurotransmission in real time.

faculty.name: Teresa Head-Gordon
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops molecular modeling techniques combining theory, simulation, and machine learning to study water structure, protein dynamics, and biomolecular interactions using multiscale simulations that bridge quantum mechanics and molecular dynamics.

faculty.name: Ke Xu
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops advanced microscopy and spectroscopy tools including single-molecule displacement/diffusivity mapping and spectrally resolved super-resolution microscopy to study biological, chemical, and materials systems at the nanoscale.

faculty.name: Jamie Cate
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how protein synthesis is regulated in human cells, particularly the role of eIF3 in controlling mRNA translation, and explores engineering bacterial ribosomes to produce novel polymers, spanning systems biology, biochemistry, and structural biology.

faculty.name: Sanjay Kumar
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates how biochemical and biomechanical inputs from extracellular matrix materials steer cell behaviors such as cancer progression and neural regeneration using engineered biomaterials and microdevices that replicate natural tissue cues.

faculty.name: Stephen Brohawn
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates ion channels and transporters that govern electrical signaling in cells, using cryo-electron microscopy, electrophysiology, and biophysics to understand the molecular basis of sensory transduction and electrical signaling.

faculty.name: Hernan Garcia
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates how single cells interpret DNA-encoded programs during development to become specialized cell types, combining physics, synthetic biology, and new technologies to query and control developmental decisions in real time at the single-cell level in the fruit fly embryo.

faculty.name: Aaron Streets
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops instruments and computational methods to study how cells interpret their genomes, applying lessons from mathematics, physics, and engineering to invent new precision measurement technology for analyzing complex biological systems at single-cell resolution.

faculty.name: Markita Landry
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops nanosensors that monitor neurotransmitter dynamics in the brain and engineers nanoscale tools for probing neural circuits, working at the intersection of nanotechnology, imaging, neurobiology, and agricultural genetic engineering.

faculty.name: Nicholas Ingolia
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how cells dynamically control mRNA translation, localization, and degradation using techniques such as ribosome profiling and APEX-seq to explore regulatory RNA-binding proteins and genetic networks influencing translational stress responses.

faculty.name: Xavier Darzacq
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops live-cell imaging methods to study the real-time dynamics of transcription factors and gene regulation at single-molecule resolution in living cells and tissues, uncovering the rules governing transcription regulation within a living cell.

faculty.name: Eunyong Park
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how intracellular organelles like the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria are built and maintained, focusing on protein transport across membranes and quality control systems using cryo-electron microscopy and structural biology.

faculty.name: Evan Miller
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops molecular tools including fluorescent dyes and genetically encoded indicators to optically track neuronal activity and map brain signal transmission with high spatial and temporal resolution, working at the interface of chemistry, cell biology, and neuroscience.

faculty.name: Karthik Shekhar
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Maps cellular heterogeneity in neural tissues using single-cell transcriptomics and studies bioelectricity through diffuse charge dynamics and electromechanics at membrane interfaces, combining chemical engineering and computational biology approaches.

faculty.name: Bronwyn Lucas
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Develops new approaches leveraging the growing compendium of atomic-resolution structures with cryo-electron microscopy to study molecular details directly within cells, bridging structural biology and cellular context.

faculty.name: Margaux Pinney
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Develops high-throughput microfluidic techniques to measure protein activity at scale, applying these methods to understand how proteins evolve new functions, how mutations cause disease and drug resistance, and how enzymes can be engineered for therapeutic use.

faculty.name: Robert Saxton
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates molecular mechanisms of immune cell signaling in the context of tissue inflammation and repair, combining protein engineering, structural biology, and functional genomics to study immune cell communication and develop treatments for chronic inflammatory diseases.

funding.note: Unknown/Verify

faculty.name: Mina Aganagic
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: String theorist working at the intersection of mathematics and physics, focusing on duality phenomena — the idea that two classically different descriptions can describe the same quantum physics. Extracts physical and mathematical predictions of dualities using sharp mathematical tests.

faculty.name: Ehud Altman
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical condensed matter physicist investigating quantum matter where interactions and quantum entanglement give rise to emergent properties. Studies many-body localization, correlated states of driven systems, ultra-cold quantum matter, and strongly correlated electronic materials including high-Tc superconductors.

faculty.name: James Analytis
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter physicist focused on discovering and understanding exotic materials manifesting novel quantum phenomena. His lab creates materials at the boundary of current understanding, studying unconventional superconductors, quantum critical systems, frustrated magnets, and topological insulators.

faculty.name: Stuart Bale
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental plasma astrophysicist developing instruments to study magnetic reconnection, collisionless shocks, plasma turbulence, and solar wind generation. Leads the Parker Solar Probe electromagnetic fields investigation and develops lunar radio telescopes for cosmic Dark Ages cosmology.

faculty.name: Raphael Bousso
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical physicist exploring the conflict between quantum mechanics and general relativity at black hole horizons, including the firewall paradox and black hole complementarity. Also works on extracting predictions from the string theory landscape, the cosmological constant problem, and the measure problem in eternal inflation.

faculty.name: Raúl Briceño
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical nuclear physicist developing computational methods in lattice QCD to understand how low-energy nuclear physics emerges from quantum chromodynamics. Bridges QCD with experimental observations from Jefferson Lab, FRIB, and the EIC, focusing on hadron spectroscopy, scattering amplitudes, and nuclear structure.

faculty.name: Carlos Bustamante
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Pioneer in single-molecule manipulation methods using optical tweezers, magnetic tweezers, and SFM cantilevers to study how mechanical forces drive biological processes. Investigates mechanochemical energy conversion in nucleic-acid-binding molecular motors, protein and RNA folding dynamics, and DNA-protein interactions in gene expression.

faculty.name: Yuan Cao
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental physicist studying electrical, optical, and mechanical properties of low-dimensional materials using nanotechnology, metamaterials, and MEMS approaches. His work on superconductivity in twisted bilayer graphene was named Physics Breakthrough of the Year in 2018.

faculty.name: Michael Crommie
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental condensed matter physicist exploring local electronic, magnetic, and mechanical properties of atomic and molecular structures at surfaces using scanned probe microscopy. Fabricates and probes nanoscale structures including carbon nanostructures, spin-based systems, molecular machines, and nano-photovoltaic interfaces.

faculty.name: Michael DeWeese
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Professor of Physics and Neuroscience exploring how stochasticity, high dimensionality, non-convex optimization, and the statistics of natural data connect across nonequilibrium statistical mechanics, machine learning theory, and systems neuroscience. Develops biologically plausible models of sensory processing and first-principles theories for deep neural networks.

faculty.name: Naomi Ginsberg
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates hierarchical materials — those whose building blocks are more complex than individual atoms — using novel electron and optical imaging techniques. Develops in situ scanning electron and cathodoluminescence microscopies, hard X-ray scattering, and ultrafast optical microscopies to study energy transport in perovskites, organic semiconductors, and photosynthetic systems.

faculty.name: Heather Gray
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental particle physicist on the ATLAS experiment at the LHC, focusing on Higgs boson interactions with different quark flavors (top, bottom, charm). Serves as Data Preparation Coordinator for ATLAS, with additional interests in track reconstruction algorithms, silicon detectors, and quantum computing algorithms.

faculty.name: Hartmut Haeffner
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses trapped ions to explore quantum mechanics, focusing on quantum simulation with ion strings, scalable quantum computing with segmented microfabricated ion traps, and ion-solid-state interactions for detecting charge carrier dynamics. Aims to interface trapped ions with Josephson-junction-based qubits.

faculty.name: William Holzapfel
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental cosmologist using cosmic microwave background measurements to constrain cosmological models and determine fundamental parameters. Leads multiple CMB experiments including BICEP (inflationary gravitational waves) and SPT (galaxy cluster surveys), with active involvement in detector and cryogenics development.

faculty.name: Petr Horava
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical physicist working on string theory as a leading candidate for quantum gravity and unification. Explores how dualities connect distinct string theories into a single framework, black hole entropy, the holographic principle, the cosmological constant problem, and how string theory modifies general relativity to be compatible with quantum mechanics.

faculty.name: Luca Victor Iliesiu
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: High-energy theorist investigating how black holes obey quantum mechanics, resolving low-temperature thermodynamics breakdown via the gravitational path integral. Also uses analytic and numerical conformal bootstrap methods to constrain the space of quantum field theories with fermionic degrees of freedom.

faculty.name: Barbara Jacak
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental nuclear physicist studying quark-gluon plasma formed in relativistic heavy ion collisions where nuclei are heated to trillions of degrees. Founding member and former spokesperson of the PHENIX experiment at RHIC at Brookhaven National Lab. Member of the National Academy of Sciences.

faculty.name: Na Ji
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops next-generation optical microscopy methods for understanding the brain at higher resolution, greater depth, and faster time scales. Applies advanced imaging technologies to study living neural systems, with broader extensions to other biological and nonliving systems.

faculty.name: Shimon Kolkowitz
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Atomic physicist and quantum scientist pioneering new techniques for ultra-precise optical atomic clocks and nanoscale sensing with atom-scale diamond defects. His group advances quantum sensing, precision measurement, and tests of fundamental physics including relativity.

faculty.name: Yury Kolomensky
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental particle physicist conducting precision tests of electroweak theory and QCD, searching for new physics at low energies. Leads efforts on the CUORE neutrinoless double-beta decay experiment, the Mu2e muon-to-electron conversion search at Fermilab, and previously BaBar at SLAC.

faculty.name: Alessandra Lanzara
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Explores the science of sustainable materials, quantum materials, and novel biological processes using coherent optical laser drives and artificial intelligence. Investigates high-temperature superconductivity, low-dimensional materials, topology and symmetry, optically driven materials, and quantum biology.

faculty.name: Harry Levine
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental physicist exploring quantum science with arrays of individual atoms, pushing quantum control limits for computation, metrology, and precision measurement. Previously developed neutral atom platforms at Harvard and hardware-efficient quantum error correction strategies at AWS Center for Quantum Computing.

faculty.name: Chung-Pei Ma
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical cosmologist and astrophysicist studying dark matter and dark energy, the cosmic microwave background, gravitational lensing, galaxy formation and evolution, supermassive black holes, and large-scale structure. Collaborates with observers using telescopes at Palomar and Keck.

faculty.name: Raffaella Margutti
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Multi-messenger astrophysicist studying violent transient phenomena including stellar explosions, neutron star and black hole mergers, and tidal disruption events. Synthesizes panchromatic observations across the entire electromagnetic spectrum with gravitational wave data; received the 2022 New Horizons in Physics Prize.

faculty.name: Benjamin Safdi
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Theoretical physicist investigating the microscopic nature of dark matter, with focus on axion dark matter. Combines experimental work (founding member of ABRACADABRA axion experiment), astrophysical probes using Fermi, Chandra, and XMM-Newton data, and high-performance computing simulations of dark matter scenarios.

funding.note: Unknown/Verify

faculty.name: Vladimir Yarov-Yarovoy
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.sub_field: Computational Structural Biology / Ion Channel Biophysics
faculty.summary: Develops computational methods (Rosetta-Membrane) for membrane protein structure prediction and design, focusing on voltage-gated ion channels (Nav, Cav, Kv). Designs subtype-specific ion channel modulators for chronic pain and epilepsy treatment.

faculty.name: Eleonora Grandi
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops biophysically detailed computational models of cardiac electrophysiology, ion transport, and signaling to understand mechanisms of heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and hypertension. Chair of the Biophysics Graduate Group.

faculty.name: Jon Sack
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates voltage-gated potassium (Kv) and sodium (Nav) channels, developing novel fluorescent probes and toxin-based tools to visualize and manipulate ion channel activity. Studies Kv2/KvS heteromers and their roles in neuronal signaling.

faculty.name: John Voss
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy to study protein misfolding and aggregation in neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's. Develops spin-labeled compounds that disrupt amyloid-beta aggregation and novel metal-free MRI contrast agents.

faculty.name: Sanjeevi Sivasankar
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies cell-cell adhesion at the single-molecule level using atomic force microscopy (AFM), focusing on cadherin proteins critical for tissue integrity. Investigates how mechanical forces regulate cadherin adhesion, with implications for cancer metastasis.

faculty.name: Tonya Kuhl
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.sub_field: Colloidal and Interface Science / Membrane Biophysics
faculty.summary: Studies biological membrane interactions, colloidal self-assembly, and complex fluids using high-resolution surface force measurements. Investigates lipid raft stability, membrane phase behavior, and targeted drug delivery systems. Chair of Chemical Engineering.

faculty.name: Atul N. Parikh
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates membrane biophysics and bioengineering, studying lipid phase separation, membrane remodeling under osmotic stress, and synthetic cell construction using giant vesicles. Develops phospholipid-based materials for biosensing and drug delivery.

faculty.name: Justin Siegel
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Combines computational tools (Rosetta Molecular Modeling Suite) with experimental validation to design novel enzymes, including those catalyzing reactions not found in nature. Develops therapeutic enzymes for celiac disease and applies AI-driven protein engineering for industrial applications.

faculty.name: Cheemeng Tan
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Engineers artificial cellular systems and synthetic cells for biosensors, drug delivery, and understanding biological principles. Develops platforms like VESSEL for engineering extracellular vesicle surface proteins and cyborg cells that are non-replicating but metabolically active.

faculty.name: R. Holland Cheng
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses cryo-electron microscopy and tomography to study macromolecular structure and function, with focus on virus assembly and infection mechanisms. Develops protein-based capsids for precision nanomedicine and mucosal delivery of CRISPR gene-editing tools.

faculty.name: Alexei A. Stuchebrukhov
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops theoretical and computational methods for biological electron and proton transfer, including the foundational Tunneling Currents method. Studies the electron transport chain, redox-driven proton pumps in Complex I and cytochrome c oxidase, and proton migration along membranes.

faculty.name: Patrice Koehl
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops computational methods at the interface of computer science, biophysics, and structural biology, including algorithms for protein shape analysis, solvation thermodynamics, and conformational transition path computation (MinActionPath). Models biomolecular electrostatics and solvation for drug design.

faculty.name: Enoch P. Baldwin
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies protein-nucleic acid interactions using X-ray crystallography and biochemistry. Investigates CTP synthetase filament formation as a novel regulatory mechanism relevant to anti-cancer drug development, and engineers TALE DNA-binding proteins for programmable DNA recognition.

faculty.name: Jawdat Al-Bassam
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies microtubule cytoskeleton structure and dynamics using cryo-electron microscopy. Mapped the structure and mechanism of tubulin cofactor complexes linked to rare childhood brain disorders (chaperone tubulinopathies), and investigates augmin-mediated microtubule branching and kinesin motor regulation.

faculty.name: Richard J. McKenney
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies molecular motor proteins (kinesins and dyneins) that transport cargo along microtubules, using single-molecule TIRF microscopy and biochemistry. Investigates how motor dysfunction leads to neurodegeneration and cancer, with focus on KIF1A-linked pediatric disorders.

faculty.name: Igor Vorobyov
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Uses molecular dynamics simulations and enhanced sampling methods to study drug interactions with ion channels and membrane proteins. Leads multi-scale modeling efforts linking atomistic simulations to cardiac cell/tissue models for predicting drug-induced arrhythmia risk.

faculty.name: Stefano Morotti
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops biophysically detailed mathematical models of cardiac and vascular myocyte electrophysiology and calcium signaling. Investigates mechanisms of heart failure and atrial fibrillation, with focus on sex-specific arrhythmia mechanisms and cross-species translational models.

faculty.name: Daisuke Sato
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies mechanisms of cardiac arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death using multiscale modeling and nonlinear dynamics. Investigates calcium cycling in cardiac myocytes, ryanodine receptor dynamics, and mechano-chemo-transduction coupling leading to arrhythmia.

faculty.name: Alla F. Fomina
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates calcium signaling and membrane trafficking in immune cells and neuroendocrine cells. Studies CRAC channels and store-operated calcium entry in T lymphocyte activation, and mechanisms of exocytosis and endocytosis in chromaffin cells.

faculty.name: Jill L. Silverman
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses rodent model systems for translational research on neurodevelopmental disorders including Angelman syndrome, autism, and pediatric epilepsy. Pioneered stem cell gene therapy approaches to reverse Angelman syndrome phenotypes, integrating behavioral phenotyping with in vivo electrophysiology.

faculty.name: Leonor Saiz
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Integrates computational biophysics, systems biology, and machine learning to study gene regulation and signal transduction networks. Models protein-DNA interactions, DNA looping, and stochastic dynamics of macromolecular assemblies using multiscale approaches from atomistic MD to cellular behavior.

faculty.name: Surl-Hee (Shirley) Ahn
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Develops and applies enhanced sampling molecular dynamics methods (weighted ensemble) as a computational microscope to study protein dynamics at atomic resolution. Investigates drug-target interactions for pain therapy and proteins implicated in Parkinson's disease.

faculty.name: Jennifer Cash
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the structural biology and regulation of Rho guanine nucleotide exchange factors (RhoGEFs) such as P-Rex1/2, which are implicated in cancer metastasis. Uses cryo-EM, X-ray crystallography, and HDX-MS to understand RhoGEF activation and identify small-molecule inhibitors.

funding.note: Unknown/Verify

faculty.name: Vassilis Angelopoulos
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Principal Investigator of NASA's THEMIS and ARTEMIS missions studying particle acceleration in Earth's magnetosphere, magnetic reconnection triggering substorms, and the lunar plasma environment. Leads the Experimental Space Physics group and the ELFIN CubeSat program training undergraduates in space hardware.

faculty.name: Marco Velli
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Observational Scientist for NASA's Parker Solar Probe mission, studying solar wind acceleration, coronal heating, magnetic switchbacks, and heliospheric current sheet dynamics. Fellow of the American Geophysical Union with research spanning MHD turbulence, wave-particle interactions, and space weather.

faculty.name: Jonathan Aurnou
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Director of the SPINLab (Simulated Planetary Interiors Laboratory), using rotating tank experiments with liquid gallium and water to study planetary dynamo action, rotating turbulence, and convective transport in planetary cores and atmospheres. Also leads the DIYnamics educational outreach project.

faculty.name: Caroline Beghein
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses earthquake and marsquake recordings to map Earth and Mars mantle structure and seismic anisotropy. NASA InSight Participating Scientist who co-detected surface waves from a 4.7 magnitude marsquake and identified a mid-mantle discontinuity on Mars using transdimensional Bayesian inversion methods.

faculty.name: Lars Stixrude
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses first-principles quantum mechanical theory (DFT), thermodynamic modeling, and machine learning potentials to understand planetary interiors from atoms to planets. Co-developed the HeFESTo code for mantle phase equilibria and studies silicate melts, magma oceans, and core formation in Earth and exoplanets. AGU Macelwane Medalist.

faculty.name: Carolina Lithgow-Bertelloni
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the connection between Earth's deep interior dynamics and surface processes including mantle convection, slab subduction, and dynamic topography. Co-developed the HeFESTo thermodynamic model for mantle minerals and demonstrated that many mantle plumes are barely hotter than surrounding mantle. AGU Union Fellow.

faculty.name: Lingsen Meng
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on earthquake source imaging, rupture dynamics, and building next-generation earthquake and tsunami early-warning systems by integrating seismic array back-projection, adjoint full-waveform inversion, numerical simulations, and AI techniques. Studies supershear ruptures, earthquake swarms, and slow slip phenomena.

faculty.name: Hao Cao
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the origin, dynamics, and impacts of planetary magnetic fields through dynamo theory, spacecraft data analysis, and numerical simulations. Involved in NASA Juno and Europa Clipper missions, with key discoveries on Jupiter's deep interior dynamics and Saturn's complex magnetic field. 2025 Sloan Research Fellow.

faculty.name: David Paige
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Principal Investigator of the Diviner Lunar Radiometer on NASA's Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter, mapping lunar surface temperatures and water ice in permanently shadowed regions since 2009. Also Deputy PI of RIMFAX ground-penetrating radar on Mars Perseverance rover and Co-I on Artemis lunar instruments. NASA Exceptional Scientific Achievement Medal recipient.

faculty.name: Jean-Luc Margot
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses radar and radio astronomy techniques to study planetary interiors, spin states, and surface properties of Venus, asteroids, and icy moons. EnVision Interdisciplinary Scientist for ESA's Venus mission and Europa Clipper gravity science team member. Also conducts radio SETI searches for technosignatures around nearby stars.

faculty.name: Jonathan Mitchell
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies atmospheric dynamics and climate of terrestrial planets, particularly Titan and Earth, using a hierarchy of climate models combined with satellite observations. Leads the TPACE group exploring transitions between Earth-like and Titan-like climates by varying planetary rotation rate, water vapor, and global water reservoir size.

faculty.name: David Jewitt
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Co-discoverer of the Kuiper Belt (Kavli Prize in Astrophysics 2012, Shaw Prize). Studies comets, Kuiper Belt objects, and active asteroids using ground-based and space telescopes, focusing on the compositional and physical properties of icy bodies and the color systematics of small solar system bodies.

faculty.name: Hilke Schlichting
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops theoretical models of planet formation and evolution at the intersection of astrophysics and planetary science. Studies super-Earths and sub-Neptunes, the core-powered mass-loss mechanism sculpting the exoplanet radius valley, magma ocean-atmosphere interactions, and planetary collisions. Asteroid 9522 named in her honor.

faculty.name: Mackenzie Day
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies wind-driven (aeolian) sediment transport and landscape evolution on Earth, Mars, and other planetary bodies through field work, wind tunnel experiments, and numerical modeling. Leads the GALE Lab investigating how dunes record climate history and reconstructing paleo-wind patterns from lithified Martian dunes. AGU Ronald Greeley Early Career Award recipient.

faculty.name: Seulgi Moon
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how tectonics, climate, and bedrock properties control landscape evolution, weathering, and erosion. Published a 2025 Science paper demonstrating that active faults weaken rocks up to ~100 km away, dramatically increasing erosional efficiency. AGU Luna B. Leopold Early Career Award and Sloan Research Fellow. Note: Moving to ETH Zurich as of February 2026 but still listed on UCLA EPSS faculty page.

faculty.name: John He
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Interdisciplinary field geologist studying lithospheric deformation and surface-crust-mantle connections through structural geology, sedimentology, geodynamic modeling, and thermochronology. Investigates lithospheric dripping beneath high-elevation plateaus and recently published a Science paper solving the mystery of the Colorado River's 5-million-year absence from the geological record.

faculty.name: Peng Ni
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental planetary scientist studying planet formation and differentiation through high-pressure/high-temperature experiments and non-traditional stable isotope geochemistry. Investigates planetesimal core formation, lunar evolution, and deep Earth processes using piston cylinder apparatus and synchrotron techniques applied to meteorites and lunar samples.

faculty.name: William Newman
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies chaos and complexity in astrophysical and geophysical systems, including relativistic magnetohydrodynamics in astrophysical jets, star and solar system formation, earthquake modeling, and climate change statistics. Holds joint appointments in Physics & Astronomy and Mathematics. Former John Simon Guggenheim Fellow and member of the Institute for Advanced Study.

funding.note: Unknown/Verify

faculty.name: Michael McNitt-Gray
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops quantitative CT imaging techniques for lung cancer screening and radiation dose estimation, including Monte Carlo simulation methods for CT dosimetry and AI-driven computer-aided diagnosis systems. His lab also contributes to open-source CT reconstruction tools (FreeCT) and imaging biomarker validation for clinical trials.

faculty.name: Magnus Dahlbom
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on nuclear medicine physics with emphasis on PET/CT imaging, theranostics, and radiopharmaceutical therapy dosimetry. His research spans PSMA-targeted molecular radiotherapy for prostate cancer, preclinical small-animal PET systems, and myocardial perfusion quantitation using PET tracers.

faculty.name: Matthew S. Brown
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops quantitative CT imaging biomarkers and AI algorithms for lung diseases including COPD, interstitial lung disease, and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. His work bridges computer vision, machine learning, and thoracic radiology to translate quantitative imaging tools into clinical practice.

faculty.name: Aichi Chien
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies intracranial aneurysm growth and rupture risk using computational fluid dynamics, MRA, and patient-specific hemodynamic analysis. Her lab also applies AI and vision-language models to cerebrovascular image analysis, including automated aneurysm detection and radiologic report summarization.

faculty.name: Anthony G. Christodoulou
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops novel MRI techniques through innovations in MR physics, machine learning, and image reconstruction. His signature MR Multitasking framework enables simultaneous multiparametric mapping in a single free-breathing scan for cardiovascular disease and cancer applications.

faculty.name: Benjamin Ellingson
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Directs the UCLA Brain Tumor Imaging Laboratory, developing and validating advanced MRI techniques to characterize brain tumors, assess treatment response, and predict patient outcomes. He co-authors consensus RANO criteria and investigates physiologic MRI biomarkers for anti-VEGF and immunotherapy response in gliomas and brain metastases.

faculty.name: Hyun Grace Kim
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on quantitative chest CT imaging and computational modeling of fibrotic lung diseases, particularly idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Combines medical imaging, statistical learning, and artificial intelligence to develop CT biomarkers that predict disease progression and treatment response.

faculty.name: Kyung Sung
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.sub_field: MRI Physics / AI in Medical Imaging
faculty.summary: Develops artificial intelligence and quantitative MRI technologies for early cancer diagnosis, treatment guidance, and therapeutic response assessment. His lab integrates deep learning with advanced image reconstruction for multiparametric prostate MRI, placental imaging, and liver perfusion MRI.

faculty.name: M. Albert Thomas
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Advances magnetic resonance spectroscopic imaging (MRSI) techniques for noninvasive metabolic assessment of tissues in health and disease. His lab develops novel MRSI methodologies and evaluates their clinical utility across brain, prostate, and other pathologies.

faculty.name: Holden Wu
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Creates and translates quantitative MRI technologies for image-guided therapy spanning detection, treatment planning, interventional guidance, and treatment monitoring of cancer and metabolic diseases. His lab develops rapid quantitative MRI for prostate, liver, and placental applications, plus real-time MRI-guided ablation and biopsy techniques.

faculty.name: Daniel Tward
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Develops computational tools for neuroimaging and brain anatomy analysis, focusing on image registration and mapping algorithms to process and interpret large-scale brain MRI datasets. His work bridges computational mathematics and clinical neuroscience.

faculty.name: Arion Chatziioannou
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Pioneers the development of high-resolution in vivo molecular imaging technology, particularly microPET and microSPECT scanners for preclinical small-animal research. His work spans detector physics, dual-modality PET/optical imaging, and automated radiochemistry systems for positron-emitting radioisotopes.

faculty.name: Johannes Czernin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Leads translational research in nuclear medicine and theranostics, with a focus on PSMA-targeted PET/CT imaging and 177Lu-PSMA radioligand therapy for prostate cancer. His work spans preclinical radiopharmaceutical development, multi-institutional clinical trials, and health policy advocacy for molecular imaging and radiotherapeutics.

faculty.name: Oluwatayo Ikotun
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Develops molecular imaging tools for in vivo characterization of immune cells, with expertise in immunoPET and radiotracer development using radionuclides such as zirconium-89. Her research spans cancer biomarker imaging, cardiovascular PET/CT, and advancing AAV gene therapy monitoring for Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

faculty.name: Jennifer Murphy
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops novel 18F-radiolabeling methodologies to create targeted PET imaging tracers for cancer, including brain-penetrant EGFR kinase inhibitors for glioblastoma imaging. Her lab designs synthetic strategies using bioorthogonal chemistry for modular, dual-modality PET/fluorescence molecular imaging agents.

faculty.name: Caius Radu
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops PET imaging approaches to monitor T lymphocyte activation in cancer and autoimmunity, bridging molecular imaging with immunology. His research focuses on creating noninvasive tools to track immune cell dynamics for improving cancer immunotherapy.

faculty.name: Michael van Dam
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops microfluidic technologies for cancer research and molecular imaging, focusing on miniaturized and automated platforms for PET radiopharmaceutical production. His lab integrates microfluidics, radiochemistry, and engineering to enable rapid, low-cost synthesis of molecular imaging probes.

faculty.name: Nzhde Agazaryan
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Serves as Chief of Clinical Medical Physics in Radiation Oncology, specializing in stereotactic radiosurgery, SBRT, small field dosimetry, IGRT, and gated radiotherapy. His research focuses on technical innovation in treatment delivery accuracy, safety, and quality assurance across brain, spine, prostate, and lung treatments.

faculty.name: Daniel Low
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Specializes in radiation oncology medical physics with emphasis on MRI-guided radiation therapy, respiratory motion modeling via 4DCT/5DCT, and stereotactic body radiotherapy. He leads multi-institutional clinical trials including MIRAGE comparing MRI-guided versus CT-guided SBRT for prostate cancer.

faculty.name: James Lamb
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Advances radiation oncology through MRI-guided radiotherapy, automated quality assurance using deep learning, and AI-assisted treatment workflow. A key contributor to the MIRAGE randomized trial, his research spans online adaptive radiotherapy, motion management, and brachytherapy for uveal melanoma.

funding.note: Unknown/Verify
12-month stipend plus fees and tuition; TA/RA/fellowships
Unknown/Verify

faculty.name: Leon Balents
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates quantum magnetism, correlated electron systems, and complex materials. Known for predicting topological insulators and Weyl semimetals, and for advancing the understanding of quantum spin liquids — exotic states of matter with macroscopic quantum entanglement of electronic spin.

faculty.name: Matthew Fisher
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the intersection of many-body theory and quantum information theory, with current focus on the non-equilibrium quantum dynamics of open and monitored quantum systems. Previously held positions at IBM, the Kavli Institute, Microsoft Station Q, and Caltech.

faculty.name: Andrea Young
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on quantum electronics in two-dimensional systems, exploring emergent electronic phenomena in van der Waals heterostructures and moiré materials.

faculty.name: Cenke Xu
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies quantum many-body systems where the interplay between quantum fluctuation and correlation effects produces diverse phenomena such as quantum criticality, non-Fermi liquids, quantum spin liquids, and topological matter, with applications to transition metal oxides, moiré heterostructures, and cold atom systems.

faculty.name: Sagar Vijay
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the intersection of quantum condensed matter physics and quantum information science, focusing on far-from-equilibrium quantum dynamics, universal traits of highly entangled quantum matter, and quantum error correction. Recipient of the Sloan Research Fellowship and the 2023 William L. McMillan Award.

faculty.name: Chenhao Jin
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Builds optical spectroscopy and imaging tools to watch quantum phenomena across space and time, exploring 2D materials, moiré superlattices, spin systems, strongly correlated materials, and topological systems, with a focus on the interplay between dimensionality, many-body interaction, fluctuations, and competing orders.

faculty.name: Ania Bleszynski Jayich
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Builds quantum technologies centered on solid-state spins, photons, and phonons to investigate foundational questions in quantum science and condensed matter physics.

faculty.name: Michel Devoret
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates quantum non-equilibrium phenomena in superconducting circuits with applications to quantum information processing, including quantum computation and quantum sensing. Pioneered the field of quantronics, where collective degrees of freedom like currents and voltages display quantum behavior.

faculty.name: John Martinis
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates superconducting devices, focusing on minimizing noise and enhancing sensitivity. His main effort involves building a quantum computer based on Josephson junction quantum bits, and he led the Google quantum supremacy experiment.

faculty.name: David Weld
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Employs ultracold quantum degenerate gases to investigate a broad spectrum of quantum dynamical phenomena, with current interests spanning quantum simulation, quantum thermodynamics, kicked quantum matter, Floquet engineering, quasicrystals, atom interferometry, and anyon synthesis.

faculty.name: David Patterson
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Extends the tools of atomic, molecular, and optical physics to prepare polyatomic molecules in single quantum states for the first time. Develops novel spectroscopy methods for ultraprecise chemical analysis, with interests spanning quantum information systems, parity violation in molecular spectra, and single-molecule chiral analysis.

faculty.name: David Berenstein
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works primarily on quantum field theories and their equivalence to quantum gravity in higher dimensions via the AdS/CFT correspondence, exploring deep connections between gauge theories and gravitational physics.

faculty.name: Nathaniel Craig
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the interface of high energy physics, particle phenomenology, and cosmology, exploring physics beyond the Standard Model through effective field theory methods and collider signatures.

faculty.name: Xi Dong
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Explores the connection between quantum entanglement and emergent spacetime through the holographic duality between string theory and quantum field theories. Also investigates foundational questions in quantum gravity and phenomenological aspects of particle physics and cosmology.

faculty.name: Steven Giddings
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works on quantum gravity, quantum field theory, black holes, quantum cosmology, and elementary particles, with a focus on fundamental questions at the intersection of general relativity and quantum mechanics.

faculty.name: David Gross
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Nobel laureate (2004) recognized for the discovery of asymptotic freedom in quantum chromodynamics. His research spans high energy physics, quantum field theory, and string theory. Permanent member of the Kavli Institute for Theoretical Physics.

faculty.name: Gary Horowitz
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on classical and quantum gravity, with particular emphasis on black holes and spacetime singularities. Member of the National Academy of Sciences and former President of the International Society on General Relativity and Gravitation.

faculty.name: Donald Marolf
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Explores black holes, gravitational aspects of string theory, and how gravity, entropy, and entanglement intersect. Investigates how bulk physics is encoded in gauge theory quantum states and studies thermal transport, quantum observables in gravity, and the firewall paradox.

faculty.name: Claudio Campagnari
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Conducts direct searches for new physics processes with the CMS experiment at the LHC, with a shift toward searches for exotic signatures such as long-lived new particles and milli-charged particles. Also performs precision Standard Model tests including measurements of rare top quark decays and Higgs boson properties.

faculty.name: Joe Incandela
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Searches for new fundamental particles at high-energy colliders. Involved in the discovery of the top quark in 1995 and the first observation of the Higgs boson in 2012. His group currently works on high-granularity silicon-based calorimeters for the CMS experiment and the LDMX light dark matter experiment.

faculty.name: David Caratelli
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Experimental particle physicist studying neutrinos to understand the fundamental laws of nature and the evolution of the universe, with work centered on the MicroBooNE and DUNE experiments investigating sterile neutrino oscillations and developing liquid argon time projection chamber technology.

faculty.name: Brendan Bowler
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates planet formation, growth, migration, and atmospheric evolution using observatories like Keck, HST, and JWST. His ultimate objective is to directly characterize terrestrial worlds in the habitable zones of their host stars in search of signs of life.

faculty.name: Benjamin Mazin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops optical/UV/X-ray Microwave Kinetic Inductance Detectors (MKIDs) and astronomical instrumentation, with a current focus on building and using MKID-based instruments for detecting and characterizing nearby exoplanets.

faculty.name: S. Peng Oh
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies galaxy formation and evolution along with the physics of diffuse plasmas, cosmic rays, turbulence, and the gaseous environments around galaxies and within galaxy clusters.

faculty.name: Crystal Martin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how galaxies assemble and how the universe transitioned from a neutral to an ionized intergalactic medium, using spectroscopy and imaging to measure properties of stars and gas in and around galaxies, applying concepts from hydrodynamics, radiative transfer, cosmology, and stellar evolution.

faculty.name: Maxwell Millar-Blanchaer
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on the direct imaging of exoplanets, brown dwarfs, and debris disks using polarimetry to detect faint objects near bright stars, combining instrument-building, large-telescope observations, and novel data analysis techniques.

faculty.name: Prateek Agrawal
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works at the intersection of particle physics and cosmology, investigating dark matter, early universe physics, and astrophysical probes of new fundamental physics.

faculty.name: Philip Lubin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies the early universe through millimeter-wavelength observations and develops applications of directed energy for planetary defense and relativistic propulsion. Co-investigator on the Planck cosmology mission and co-recipient of the 2006 and 2018 Gruber Prizes in Cosmology.

funding.note: Full financial support to all admitted PhD students

faculty.name: David Awschalom
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies understanding and controlling the spins of individual electrons, ions, and nuclei for fundamental studies of quantum phenomena, with applications in computing, sensing, imaging, and encryption. Investigates electron spin coherence and the spin Hall effect in semiconductors.

faculty.name: Edward Blucher
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Conducts experimental particle physics research at Fermilab, including leadership in the PIP-II accelerator project. Serves as Director of the Enrico Fermi Institute and was elected a 2021 AAAS Fellow.

faculty.name: Marcela Carena
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works on theoretical particle physics phenomenology at the interface of theory and experiment at Fermilab. Chairs the APS Division of Particles and Fields and was named a 2022 DOE Distinguished Scientist Fellow for contributions to particle physics roadmap planning.

faculty.name: John E. Carlstrom
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Conducts observational cosmology research as Subramanyan Chandrasekhar Distinguished Service Professor, with key involvement in the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration that was awarded the 2020 Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics. Leads cosmic microwave background studies through the South Pole Telescope.

faculty.name: Cheng Chin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Explores ultracold quantum systems including Bose-Einstein condensation, strongly interacting Fermi gases, and quantum simulation of phenomena ranging from condensed matter to cosmology. Recent work covers transitions from atomic to molecular BEC and quantum simulation of Unruh radiation.

faculty.name: Juan I. Collar
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on detector development for dark matter and neutrino physics experiments, searching for weakly interacting massive particles and measuring neutrino properties through rare-event detection techniques. Has contributed to public understanding of dark matter through educational explainers.

faculty.name: Clay Córdova
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on theoretical physics with a focus on symmetry and quantum field theory. Recipient of the New Horizons in Physics Prize and a Sloan Research Fellowship, recognizing groundbreaking contributions to theoretical high energy physics.

faculty.name: Luca Delacrétaz
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Studies collective and emergent phenomena in condensed matter physics through the lens of quantum field theory. Applies QFT frameworks to understand how complex, large-scale behaviors arise from many-particle systems.

faculty.name: Margaret Gardel
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates the physical mechanics of living cells and cytoskeletal systems through her lab. Her research spans biophysics and soft matter, with work on cell division mechanisms and the material properties of biological tissues. Serves as Director of the James Franck Institute and the Center for Living Systems.

faculty.name: Luca Grandi
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Leads direct dark matter detection efforts using liquid xenon time projection chambers, including the XENONnT and XENON1T experiments. Recently achieved the first measurement of nuclear recoil signals from solar neutrinos with XENONnT.

faculty.name: Philippe Guyot-Sionnest
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses colloidal synthesis to create nanostructures and investigates quantum states, charging, spin, phonons, and plasmons at the nanometer scale. His research is driven by physical concepts and enabled by chemical synthesis, bridging physics and chemistry.

faculty.name: Keisuke Harigaya
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates mysteries confronting particle physics by constructing models and generating testable predictions. His research spans the strong CP problem, axion cosmology, dark matter, baryogenesis, grand unification, electroweak symmetry breaking, and inflation, with experimental tests possible across colliders, dark matter detectors, axion searches, and cosmological observations.

faculty.name: Daniel Holz
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies how detecting gravitational waves from merging black holes and neutron stars can reveal the physics and expansion history of the universe. A LIGO collaborator who helped discover GW150914 and GW170817, he works on using gravitational-wave standard sirens to constrain cosmology.

faculty.name: William T.M. Irvine
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates turbulence and related nonlinear phenomena in fluid and soft matter systems. His research explores fundamental questions about how chaotic flows organize and how topological structures emerge in fluid dynamics.

faculty.name: Heinrich M. Jaeger
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates granular materials and dense suspensions far from equilibrium, exploring phenomena such as shear-induced jamming and ultrasonic levitation assembly. His work aims to design new classes of smart materials for applications in energy absorption, soft robotics, and recyclable structures.

faculty.name: Young-Kee Kim
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Researches the origin of mass for fundamental particles — the Higgs mechanism — using the world's highest-energy colliders (TRISTAN, Tevatron, LHC). Her group has led precision measurements of W and top-quark masses and Higgs decay properties, and she also investigates novel accelerator concepts including AI-driven approaches to overcome beam-intensity limitations.

faculty.name: Michael Levin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies topological phases of matter that lack conventional symmetry-breaking order, developing new conceptual tools to understand them. Also investigates which quantum many-body systems can be efficiently simulated on a classical computer and the role of entanglement in many-body ground states.

faculty.name: Nadya Mason
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Conducts experimental studies of quantum materials, exploring nano-scale wires, atomically thin membranes, and nanostructured superconductors. Her work has relevance for nanoscale and quantum computing elements, including strain-induced pseudomagnetic fields in graphene and Josephson diode effects.

faculty.name: David W. Miller
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies quarks and gluons produced in proton collisions at the Large Hadron Collider using the ATLAS detector to search for new particles and characterize known forces with greater precision. His work on jet signatures contributed to the Higgs boson discovery and searches for new massive particles decaying into top quarks.

faculty.name: Arvind Murugan
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Explores error correction mechanisms and design principles in biological systems using theoretical and computational approaches. Recipient of the 2025 Tel Aviv University International Prize in Biophysics and named a 2025 Schmidt Polymath.

faculty.name: Stephanie Palmer
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Applies theoretical and computational approaches to understand how brain cells organize and connect, bridging physics and organismal biology. Named a 2025 Schmidt Science Polymath awardee for her interdisciplinary neuroscience research.

faculty.name: Dam Thanh Son
faculty.title: University Professor
faculty.summary: Applies gauge-gravity duality from string theory to strongly interacting systems including the quark-gluon plasma, quantum Hall systems, graphene, and the BCS-BEC crossover in cold atoms. His work bridges high energy theory with condensed matter and nuclear physics.

faculty.name: Abigail Vieregg
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Builds radio and millimeter wave experiments for ultra-high-energy neutrino detection and CMB cosmology. Serves as PI of the PUEO balloon-borne and RNO-G ground-based radio neutrino detectors, while also contributing to BICEP, the South Pole Telescope, and the future CMB-S4 experiment.

faculty.name: Scott Wakely
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates very-high-energy cosmic radiation including gamma rays and cosmic rays above 1 GeV. Works on three major experimental efforts: the balloon-borne HELIX experiment measuring cosmic-ray isotopes, the VERITAS telescope array detecting gamma rays from tens of GeV to tens of TeV, and the next-generation CTA observatory.

faculty.name: Zoe Yan
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Studies quantum many-body systems using ultracold atoms and molecules, employing advanced trapping and imaging techniques to build tailored Hamiltonians for quantum simulation. Joined UChicago in August 2023 after a postdoctoral fellowship at Princeton and a Ph.D. from MIT.

funding.note: Fully funded PhD

faculty.name: Tobin R. Sosnick
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Sosnick lab integrates experimental (HX, NMR, SAXS, mass spectrometry) and computational (molecular dynamics, coarse-grain simulations) approaches to study protein and RNA folding, function, and design. Recent work explores transcriptome-wide mRNP condensation in stress granule formation, temperature-dependent fold-switching in the circadian clock protein KaiB, and the design of light-sensitive allosteric proteins for optogenetics.

faculty.name: Erin J. Adams
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Adams lab investigates the molecular signals the immune system uses to distinguish healthy from unhealthy tissue, focusing on unconventional T cell recognition involving γδ T cells, NKT cells, and MAIT cells alongside antigen presentation by nonclassical MHC-like proteins. The group integrates biochemistry, structural biology, protein engineering, and cellular assays to uncover how immune effector cells regulate human disease.

faculty.name: Eduardo Perozo
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Perozo lab studies the structure, function, and gating mechanisms of ion channels, including potassium channels (Shaker, HERG, BK), mechanosensitive channels (MscS), and voltage-sensing domains. The group uses structural biology, electrophysiology, and biophysical approaches to understand how lipid bilayer deformations drive channel gating and how conformation-specific tools can discriminate functional states.

faculty.name: Robert J. Keenan
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Keenan lab investigates the molecular mechanisms underlying membrane protein biogenesis and quality control using biochemical reconstitution, structural biology (cryo-EM), and cell biology approaches. Recent work focuses on the ribosome-translocon complex, regulated N-glycosylation at the secretory translocon, and unifying models for how multi-pass membrane proteins are built and folded at the ER.

faculty.name: Benoit Roux
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Roux lab bridges theoretical/computational chemistry and experimental biochemistry to understand molecular mechanisms of membrane proteins, especially ion channels. The group develops and applies molecular simulation tools (notably the CHARMM software package) to study channel gating, ion permeation, and inactivation processes at atomic resolution, while also working on antibody polyreactivity characterization and biosensor design.

faculty.name: Joseph A. Piccirilli
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Piccirilli lab investigates RNA structure, function, and enzymology, with a focus on ribozyme mechanisms, tRNA modifications and their roles in mitochondrial pathology, and RNA-small molecule interactions. The group employs chemical biology, biochemistry, and structural approaches to understand RNA conformational dynamics in disease contexts and the fundamental chemistry of RNA catalysis.

faculty.name: Phoebe A. Rice
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Rice lab investigates how mobile genetic elements move using biochemical, structural, and microbiological approaches. Current work focuses on the SCCmec element of MRSA, the paradigmatic Mu transposase, large serine integrases and their directionality control, and transpososome structures, with implications for understanding horizontal gene transfer and microbial pathogenesis.

faculty.name: Anthony A. Kossiakoff
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Kossiakoff Group investigates how molecular recognition drives biological function, combining structural methods (crystallography, cryo-EM) with protein engineering tools. A major focus is developing synthetic antibodies and chaperone-assisted crystallography to tackle protein systems recalcitrant to conventional approaches, with applications spanning mycobacterial cell wall biosynthesis, mTOR signaling, chemokine receptor-arrestin interactions, and apoptosis.

faculty.name: Rama Ranganathan
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Ranganathan lab studies the design principles underlying structure, function, and adaptation in biological systems from atomic to cellular scales. The group uses coevolutionary analysis, deep learning-guided protein design, time-resolved crystallography, and systems biology approaches to understand how complex biological functions — from protein folding to allosteric regulation to signaling networks — arise through evolutionary processes.

faculty.name: Tao Pan
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Pan lab studies tRNA biology and epitranscriptomics, focusing on how tRNA dynamics and mRNA modifications like m6A regulate gene expression. The group developed high-throughput sequencing methods to measure tRNA abundance, charging, and modifications, discovered the m6A switch mechanism, and investigates how the gut microbiome reprograms host m6A modifications in a tissue-dependent manner.

faculty.name: Marcos Sotomayor
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Sotomayor lab integrates computational structural biology, simulations, and biochemistry to study how mechanical forces are detected and transduced at the molecular level. A central focus is the auditory hair-cell mechanotransduction complex, including TMC1/2 channels and cadherin-based tip-link proteins, with additional work on TMEM63 mechanosensitive channels and the biophysics of lipid scrambling.

faculty.name: Marvin W. Makinen
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Makinen lab focuses on vanadium-based compounds for cancer imaging and therapy, developing vanadium-48-labeled vanadyl acetylacetonate as a novel PET imaging agent. Research also investigates vanadyl chelates as inhibitors of protein tyrosine phosphatase-1B, an enzyme regulating insulin and leptin signaling linked to breast, colorectal, and ovarian cancer.

faculty.name: Chuan He
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The He lab investigates how reversible RNA and DNA methylation regulate gene expression and cellular processes. Discovered the first RNA demethylase (FTO), launching the field of epitranscriptomics, and characterized the m6A methyltransferase complex and reader proteins. Recent work explores RNA modification systems as therapeutic targets in leukemia, microbiome-derived cell-free RNA modifications for colorectal cancer detection, and m6A readers in antitumor immunity.

faculty.name: Joseph W. Thornton
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: The Thornton lab investigates how protein functions evolve over deep time using phylogenetic reconstruction of ancestral proteins followed by synthesis and experimental characterization. The group tackles questions such as how complex molecular systems evolve, the role of epistasis, whether evolution is reversible, and how protein architecture itself evolves, combining evolutionary biology, biochemistry, biophysics, and computational biology.

faculty.name: D. Allan Drummond
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: The Drummond lab investigates how cells cope with stress at the molecular level, focusing on the formation and dissolution of large assemblies of proteins and RNA during events like heat shock. The group integrates in vivo imaging, in vitro reconstitution, mechanistic biochemistry, quantitative proteomics, and molecular evolutionary analyses, primarily using budding yeast as a model organism to study stress-triggered biomolecular condensation as an adaptive, evolutionarily tuned response.

faculty.name: Jingyi Fei
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: The Fei lab investigates how RNAs govern gene expression across bacterial and eukaryotic systems using advanced single-molecule and super-resolution imaging. Recent work has explored transcriptome-wide mRNA condensation preceding stress granule formation, RNA localization dynamics at nuclear speckles linked to splicing efficiency, translational T-box riboswitch mechanisms, and KARR-seq for mapping cellular higher-order RNA structures.

faculty.name: Engin Ozkan
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: The Ozkan lab investigates the biochemistry and interaction networks of proteins essential for nervous system development, focusing on deciphering protein-protein interaction networks of the extracellular space required for axon guidance, pruning, and synapse formation. The group employs a multidisciplinary approach combining crystallography, cellular signaling assays, and protein engineering with genetic modifications in model organisms.

faculty.name: Minglei Zhao
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: The Zhao lab studies the mechanisms of molecular machines and their roles in human disease, focusing on two primary systems: the p97/ubiquitination system (a central hub in cellular protein homeostasis linked to neurodegenerative diseases and cancer) and the vault RNP particle (the largest ribonucleoprotein in eukaryotes whose function remains elusive). The group uses structural, proteomic, and imaging approaches to elucidate molecular mechanisms.

faculty.name: Demet Arac-Ozkan
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: The Arac lab investigates how cell-surface receptors mediate intercellular adhesion and communication, focusing on their roles in brain development, synapse formation, and neural wiring. Using structural biology and biochemical approaches, the group studies adhesion GPCRs (ADGRG6/GPR126, ADGRL3/Latrophilin3, CELSR1-3) with direct relevance to neurological disorders, developmental impairments, glioblastoma, and congenital anosmia.

faculty.name: Ronald S. Rock
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: The Rock lab investigates how myosin motor proteins navigate the cytoskeleton and dynamically organize cells, studying biological motion from single-molecule to cellular scales. The group uses advanced fluorescence microscopy, optical tweezers, and structural biology to observe myosin movement in vitro and in living cells, and has developed optogenetic tools like LILAC for enhanced actin imaging.

faculty.name: Cassandra Hayne
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: The Hayne lab investigates how tRNAs are processed and regulated, focusing on the making and breaking of tRNAs by ribonucleases, with implications for neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental disorders. Using cryo-electron microscopy, the group has elucidated the structural basis of pre-tRNA recognition and cleavage by the human TSEN (tRNA splicing endonuclease) complex.

faculty.name: Axel Concepcion
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: The Concepcion lab identifies and characterizes novel membrane proteins and ion channels in immune cells, combining functional genomics, systems immunology, electrophysiology, and cell biology to understand how ion channels regulate immune responses. Research has implications for viral infection, autoimmunity, and cancer, with recent work funded by the NIH and Cancer Research Foundation.
