faculty.name: Amlan Biswas
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies transport and surface properties of magnetic and superconducting oxides, focusing on manganite thin films, phase separation, multiferroic materials, colossal piezoresistance, and tunneling magnetoresistance in complex oxide systems.

faculty.name: Stephen Hagen
faculty.title: Professor and Chair
faculty.summary: Investigates biological physics with emphasis on gene regulatory networks, bacterial signaling, communication, and information processing, including quorum sensing and competence regulation in bacterial populations.

faculty.name: Peter Hirschfeld
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Theorist studying unconventional superconductors including heavy fermion materials, high-temperature cuprates, and iron-based pnictides/chalcogenides, focusing on multiorbital physics, gap symmetry, and disorder effects on the superconducting state.

faculty.name: Sergey Klimenko
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Researches gravitational-wave detection with LIGO and multi-messenger astronomy. Developed the Coherent WaveBurst search algorithm that discovered the first binary black hole merger signal, enabling real-time detection critical for electromagnetic follow-up of GW events.

faculty.name: Andrey Korytov
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Focuses on Higgs boson physics with precision measurements following its 2012 discovery at the LHC, searches for physics beyond the Standard Model including Supersymmetry, and works on the CMS experiment muon system and muon reconstruction.

faculty.name: James Hamlin
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Combines materials synthesis, characterization, and high-pressure techniques (from kilobars to megabars) to study novel electronic and magnetic materials, focusing on understanding conditions under which unconventional and high-temperature superconducting states form.

faculty.name: Dmitrii Maslov
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works on correlated electron systems including Fermi-liquid theory and quantum phase transitions, spin-orbit interaction in solids, and new phases of matter arising under strong magnetic fields.

faculty.name: Guido Mueller
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Works on terrestrial and space-based gravitational wave observatories (LIGO, LISA) focusing on input optics and advanced interferometer designs, and also develops detectors for axions and axion-like particles with the ALPS collaboration.

faculty.name: Heather Ray
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Uses neutrinos created at particle accelerators and neutron sources to search for sterile neutrinos and light dark matter, exploring physics beyond the Standard Model through neutrino experiments.

faculty.name: Tarek Saab
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Leads the SuperCDMS collaboration searching for dark matter WIMPs with cryogenic detectors at SNOLAB, and also pursues detection of light dark matter candidates using superfluid liquid helium with nano electro-mechanical system oscillators.

faculty.name: Christopher Stanton
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Develops theory of ultrafast optical, electrical, and transport properties of bulk and quantum confined semiconductors, including coherent carrier kinetics, THz radiation from ultrafast excitation, and coherent optical and acoustic phonons in nanostructures.

faculty.name: David Tanner
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.sub_field: Dark Matter / Gravitational Waves / Condensed Matter Experiment
faculty.summary: Contributes to the Axion Dark Matter eXperiment (ADMX) searching for axions as dark matter candidates, leads UF's LIGO input optics group for gravitational-wave detection, and studies optical properties of solids including high-temperature superconductors and conducting polymers.

faculty.name: Clifford M. Will
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Works on the theory of gravitational radiation from astrophysical sources, tests of general relativity in the solar system and binary pulsars, and studies relativistic effects of massive black holes including tests of black hole no-hair theorems.

faculty.name: Richard Woodard
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Research spans particle physics, inflationary cosmology, and quantum gravity, affiliated with the Institute for Fundamental Theory at UF.

faculty.name: Kevin Ingersent
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Studies strongly correlated electron systems, focusing on non-Fermi-liquid metallic behavior arising near absolute zero from bulk quantum phase transitions or transitions in magnetic nanostructures such as quantum-dot devices, emphasizing competition among conduction electrons, magnetic interactions, and electromagnetic fluctuations.

faculty.name: Xiao-Xiao Zhang
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Probes light-matter interaction and transient dynamics in nanoscale materials by combining novel 2D device fabrication with all-optical control capabilities, investigating monolayer semiconductors, magnetic crystals, topological materials, and magnetic molecules.

faculty.name: Imre Bartos
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies extreme cosmic explosions related to the formation and evolution of black holes through multi-messenger astrophysics, combining signals from gravitational waves, neutrinos, and gamma-rays to understand astrophysical events.

faculty.name: Laura Blecha
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Develops theoretical models and predictions for supermassive black hole and galaxy evolution using gravitational, hydrodynamics, and radiative transfer simulations from galactic to cosmological scales, collaborating with observers and the Illustris simulation collaboration.

faculty.name: Paul Fulda
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Studies optical characteristics of Advanced LIGO detectors using frequency domain modal simulations to enhance sensitivity, performs experimental R&D for future LIGO upgrades, and works on LISA's optical metrology system including telescope prototype testing.

faculty.name: BingKan Xue
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Investigates biological form and function through a physics lens, focusing on biological adaptation, epigenetic inheritance, and developmental plasticity using methods from statistical physics, information theory, and machine learning.

faculty.name: Wei Xue
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Works on understanding the nature of dark matter through multiple approaches: using LHC and future collider data to search for beyond-Standard-Model physics, exploring novel dark matter models and their cosmological histories, and using galaxies and stars as laboratories for fundamental physics.

faculty.name: Jeff Andrews
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Studies binary populations, stellar interactions, dynamics of stellar systems, and formation of neutron star and black hole binaries, using astrometric data from Gaia to detect and measure hidden stellar companions.

faculty.name: Jeff Dror
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Searches for new cosmic fossils in the form of axions and gravitational waves to understand the Universe's evolution, investigating axions as dark matter or dark radiation candidates and studying nHz-range gravitational waves from early-Universe events via pulsar timing data analysis.

faculty.name: Dominique Laroche
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Fabricates nano-circuits and characterizes them at cryogenic temperatures through resonant tunneling and Coulomb drag measurements, focusing on strong electron-electron interactions in one-dimensional Luttinger liquids and Majorana zero modes in hybrid superconductor-semiconductor devices.

faculty.name: Edwin Pedrozo-Peñafiel
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Develops cavity quantum electrodynamics platforms for non-destructive measurements and collective light-matter interactions, creating entangled states for sensing beyond the standard quantum limit and using ultracold atoms in optical cavities to benchmark quantum measurement techniques.
